---
format: policy-brief-typst
series: "Example Policy Initiative"
issue: "Brief No. 4"
date: "2026-04-01"
title: "Staffing Community Services"
subtitle: "Why population is the wrong metric — and what to use instead"
brief-author: "Jane Doe, Ph.D."
brief-affiliation: "Example Institute, Example University"
key-findings:
  - "Service offices face capacity constraints that are poorly predicted by population alone."
  - "Staffing ratios, site contiguity, and distance to the central office together explain over 60% of variation in wait times and service denials."
  - "A three-variable capacity index better identifies at-risk jurisdictions than the population thresholds used in most federal formulas."
cite-as: "Doe, Jane. 2026. \"Staffing Community Services.\" Example Policy Initiative Brief No. 4. Example Institute, Example University."
contact: "example-institute@example.edu  ·  example-institute.example.edu"
# accent-color: "1a5490"    # optional — hex without the #
---

# The problem

Federal and state programs that allocate resources to local service
offices typically use **county population** as the primary measure of
need. Yet jurisdictions with similar populations vary substantially in
their capacity to deliver services — and the variation matters for the
people those programs are meant to reach. This brief draws on an
original survey of 412 local officials and administrative records from
two recent fiscal years to show why a better metric is needed, and what
one could look like.

```{=typst}
#pullquote[
  Two jurisdictions with identical populations can have service wait
  times that differ by a factor of four. Population is not capacity.
]
```

# What we measured

We combined three indicators that officials themselves identified as
primary constraints: the ratio of permanent staff to the eligible
population, the geographic contiguity of service sites within a
jurisdiction, and the average distance from sites to the central office.
Each is straightforward to measure from publicly available
administrative data.

## Staffing ratios

Offices with fewer than one permanent staff member per 5,000 eligible
residents were three times more likely to report service-denial rates
above the state median. The effect is concentrated in smaller
jurisdictions — where a single retirement or medical leave can halve
administrative capacity.

## Site contiguity and distance

Service sites are the operational units that residents interact with.
In the 200 most geographically dispersed jurisdictions we studied, the
average site was 22 miles from the central office, and in 38
jurisdictions at least one site was more than 50 miles away. That
distance shows up in wait times, in equipment deployment costs, and in
the likelihood that problems encountered in the field can be resolved
before the close of business.

# Why population misses this

Two jurisdictions with 20,000 eligible residents each can have
radically different administrative profiles: one may have a single site
and two permanent staff, the other may have 14 sites spread across 800
square miles and one half-time coordinator. Population treats them as
the same. Our proposed three-variable index treats them differently —
and more accurately predicts the outcomes policymakers care about.

```{=typst}
#recommendations[
  - **Replace population-only thresholds** in federal allocation
    formulas with a three-variable capacity index.
  - **Fund state-level capacity audits** that inventory staffing, site
    contiguity, and geographic dispersion at the jurisdiction level.
  - **Tie technical-assistance resources** — training, equipment,
    peer-network programs — to the bottom quartile of the capacity
    index rather than to population thresholds.
]
```

# What's next

We are currently working with the state coordinating agency to pilot
capacity audits in a set of jurisdictions during the next fiscal year.
Results will inform a longer report planned for release in late 2026.
